questions.q: What is Amortized Time Complexity? Explain with a Dynamic Array.
questions.a:
Amortized analysis gives the average cost per operation over a sequence of operations, even if a single operation occasionally takes much longer.

Dynamic Array (e.g., Python `list`, Java `ArrayList`):
Appending an item normally takes O(1) time. However, when the underlying fixed-size array is full, the system must allocate a new array of double the size (2N) and copy all N elements over. This specific resize operation takes O(N) time.

But how often does this happen?
If we start with size 1, we resize at sizes 1, 2, 4, 8, 16...
The total cost of copying for N insertions is `1 + 2 + 4 + ... + N/2 + N`.
This is a geometric series that sums to `2N`.
Therefore, the total cost for N insertions is `N (for the actual appends) + 2N (for copying) = 3N`.
Dividing the total work (3N) by N operations gives an Amortized Time Complexity of O(1) per append.
questions.trap: Do not confuse Amortized Time with Average Case Time. Average Case is based on the probability of random inputs. Amortized is a strict mathematical guarantee over a continuous sequence of operations.

questions.q: What is the difference between a Tree, a Binary Tree, and a Binary Search Tree (BST)?
questions.a:
Tree: A connected, acyclic graph consisting of nodes and edges, with a single root node. A node can have any number of children.

Binary Tree: A tree where every node has at most two children (Left and Right). No specific ordering of values is required.

Binary Search Tree (BST): A Binary Tree that strictly enforces a sorting property: For any given node, ALL values in its Left subtree must be strictly less than the node's value, and ALL values in its Right subtree must be greater.
This property allows for O(log N) search, insertion, and deletion times (assuming the tree is balanced).
cross: What happens if you insert already sorted data into a standard BST? → It degrades into a linked list (a straight line of right children), making search O(N). This is why self-balancing trees (AVL, Red-Black) are necessary.

questions.q: What is a Heap (Priority Queue)? Explain its operations.
questions.a:
A Heap is a Complete Binary Tree (filled top-to-bottom, left-to-right) that satisfies the Heap Property.
— Max-Heap: Every parent node is ≥ its children. (The root is the absolute maximum).
— Min-Heap: Every parent node is ≤ its children. (The root is the absolute minimum).

Array Representation: Because it is complete, it is always stored as a flat array to save memory. For a node at index `i`:
— Left child = `2i + 1`
— Right child = `2i + 2`
— Parent = `(i - 1) / 2`

Operations:
— Peek: O(1) (just read array[0]).
— Insert: O(log N). Add to the end of the array, then 'Bubble Up' (swap with parent until the heap property is restored).
— Extract-Max/Min: O(log N). Remove the root, move the very last element of the array to the root, then 'Bubble Down' (swap with the largest/smallest child).
cross: What is Heapify? → The process of converting an unsorted array into a valid Heap. It uniquely takes O(N) time, NOT O(N log N), if done bottom-up.

questions.q: Explain BFS vs DFS in Graphs/Trees. When to use which?
questions.a:
Breadth-First Search (BFS):
Explores level-by-level, radiating outward from the start node.
— Data Structure: Queue (FIFO).
— Use Cases: Finding the Shortest Path in an unweighted graph, finding all friends within 2 degrees of connection, level-order tree traversal.
— Space Complexity: O(W), where W is the maximum width of the tree/graph. Bad for very wide graphs.

Depth-First Search (DFS):
Explores as far down one path as possible before backtracking.
— Data Structure: Stack (LIFO) or Recursion.
— Use Cases: Cycle detection, Topological Sorting, solving Mazes, counting Connected Components.
— Space Complexity: O(H), where H is the maximum depth. Bad for infinitely deep graphs (can cause StackOverflow).
questions.trap: For graph traversal, NEVER forget to maintain a `visited` Set (Hash Set). Without it, a cycle in the graph will cause an infinite loop.

questions.q: Explain Graph Representations: Adjacency Matrix vs Adjacency List.
questions.a:
Adjacency Matrix: A 2D array (V x V) where `matrix[i][j] = 1` if an edge exists between node i and node j.
— Space Complexity: O(V²). Terrible for sparse graphs (wastes huge memory on 0s).
— Time to check if edge exists: O(1). Fast.
— Time to find all neighbors of a node: O(V). Slow, must scan the whole row.

Adjacency List: An Array (or HashMap) of Lists. `list[i]` contains a linked list of all neighbors of node i.
— Space Complexity: O(V + E). Highly efficient for sparse graphs (most real-world graphs like social networks or maps).
— Time to check if edge exists: O(Degree of V). Slower.
— Time to find all neighbors: O(Degree of V). Extremely fast, just iterate the list.
questions.trap: In interviews, default to Adjacency List unless the problem explicitly involves a dense graph or requires instant edge-checking.

questions.q: What is Topological Sort? When is it used?
questions.a:
Topological Sorting is a linear ordering of vertices such that for every directed edge `U -> V`, vertex `U` comes strictly before `V` in the ordering.

Constraint: It is ONLY possible on a DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph). If the graph contains a cycle, topological sorting is mathematically impossible.

Use Cases:
— Task scheduling with prerequisites (e.g., 'You must take CS101 before CS201').
— Package managers (NPM/Maven) resolving build dependencies.
— Determining the compilation order of source files.

Algorithms:
1. Kahn's Algorithm (BFS): Calculate the 'in-degree' (number of incoming edges) for all nodes. Push all nodes with 0 in-degree to a Queue. Pop, add to result, and decrement the in-degree of all neighbors. Repeat.
2. DFS: Run standard DFS. When a node finishes exploring ALL its neighbors, push it to a Stack. Pop the stack at the end.

All are algorithms to find Shortest Paths in weighted graphs.

1. Dijkstra's Algorithm:
— Finds shortest path from one Source node to all other nodes.
— Uses a Min-Heap (Priority Queue) greedily choosing the closest unvisited node.
— Time: `O((V + E) log V)`. Extremely fast.
— Fatal Flaw: Fails completely if the graph contains Negative Weight Edges.

2. Bellman-Ford:
— Also Single-Source shortest path.
— Relaxes ALL edges exactly `V - 1` times.
— Time: `O(V * E)`. Slower.
— Advantage: Can handle Negative Weights. Furthermore, if it relaxes edges on a `V`th iteration, it successfully detects Negative Weight Cycles.

3. Floyd-Warshall:
— All-Pairs shortest path (finds shortest path between EVERY pair of nodes simultaneously).
— Uses Dynamic Programming (a 3D matrix).
— Time: `O(V³)`. Only viable for very small graphs.

questions.q: What is a Trie (Prefix Tree)? Why use it over a HashMap?
questions.a:
A Trie is a highly specialized tree data structure used for storing strings. Each node represents a single character. A path from the root to a node marked `isEndOfWord` represents a complete word.

Why use it over a HashMap (HashSet)?
If you want to check if the exact word "apple" exists, a HashSet is `O(L)` (where L is the length of the word to compute the hash). A Trie is also `O(L)`.

HOWEVER, HashSets cannot efficiently answer Prefix Queries. If you want to find "All words that start with 'app'", a HashSet requires you to iterate through every single word in the dictionary.
A Trie can jump directly to the 'p' node in `O(L)` time, and then run a simple DFS to find all valid words branching from there.

Use Cases:
Search Engine Autocomplete, Spell Checkers, IP Routing Longest-Prefix-Match, Boggle/Scrabble board solvers.

questions.q: Explain Dynamic Programming: Memoization vs Tabulation.
questions.a:
Dynamic Programming (DP) optimizes exponential recursive algorithms (like `O(2^N)`) down to polynomial time (like `O(N)`) by caching the results of Overlapping Subproblems.

1. Memoization (Top-Down):
— You write a natural Recursive function.
— Before computing a subproblem, check if the result exists in a Cache (HashMap/Array). If yes, return it. If no, compute it, store it in the Cache, and return it.
— Pros: Intuitive to write. Only computes subproblems that are strictly necessary (Lazy Evaluation).
— Cons: Function call stack overhead. Can cause `StackOverflowError` on deep recursion.

2. Tabulation (Bottom-Up):
— You write an Iterative function using loops.
— You build an Array (or Matrix) starting from the base cases (`dp[0]`, `dp[1]`) and use them to calculate the next step (`dp[2]`), all the way up to the target `dp[n]`.
— Pros: No recursion overhead. Extremely fast constant factors. Perfect cache locality.
— Cons: Forces you to compute ALL subproblems up to N, even if some aren't needed.

questions.q: What is the Sliding Window technique?
questions.a:
The Sliding Window is a subset of the Two-Pointer technique used to solve array/string problems involving contiguous subarrays or substrings, reducing `O(N²)` nested loops down to `O(N)` time.

How it works:
You maintain two pointers, `Left` and `Right`, which define a 'Window' of valid elements.
1. Expand the window by moving `Right` and adding the new element to your current state (e.g., sum, hashmap counts).
2. Check if the window violates the problem's constraint (e.g., 'sum is > target' or 'duplicate character found').
3. If invalid, shrink the window by moving `Left` forward, removing elements from your state until the window becomes valid again.
4. Keep track of the maximum/minimum window size found.

Classic Problems: Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters, Maximum Sum Subarray of Size K, Minimum Window Substring.

questions.q: What is Backtracking? How is it different from DFS/DP?
questions.a:
Backtracking is an algorithmic paradigm used to find all (or some) solutions to computational problems by incrementally building candidates. If a candidate is determined to be invalid, the algorithm Abandons it (prunes the branch) and 'Backtracks' to the previous step to try a different path.

Standard Template:
1. Choose: Add an option to the current state.
2. Explore: Recursively call the function.
3. Un-Choose (Backtrack): Remove the option from the current state to try the next loop iteration.

Difference from DFS: Backtracking uses DFS to traverse the decision tree, but specifically includes the 'Un-Choose' state-reversion step.
Difference from DP: DP finds a single optimal value by caching overlapping subproblems. Backtracking generates ALL possible valid combinations/permutations. Backtracking problems rarely have overlapping subproblems, so caching doesn't help.

Classic Problems: N-Queens, Sudoku Solver, Generate all Permutations/Subsets, Word Search.

questions.q: Explain the Disjoint Set (Union-Find) data structure.
questions.a:
Union-Find is a highly specialized data structure used to keep track of a set of elements partitioned into a number of disjoint (non-overlapping) subsets. It is the absolute best algorithm for finding Connected Components in an undirected graph or detecting cycles.

Core Operations:
1. Find(x): Determines which subset an element belongs to. (Usually returns a 'Root' representative node).
2. Union(x, y): Merges two subsets into a single subset.

Crucial Optimizations:
— Path Compression: During `Find(x)`, make every node visited along the path point directly to the Root. This flattens the tree, making future lookups instant.
— Union by Rank: During `Union`, always attach the smaller tree under the root of the taller tree to keep the overall tree shallow.

With both optimizations, operations run in O(α(N)) time, where `α` is the Inverse Ackermann function. For all practical values of N, `α(N) <= 4`, meaning operations are effectively O(1) constant time.

Use Case: Kruskal's Minimum Spanning Tree algorithm.

A Segment Tree is a binary tree used for storing information about intervals or segments. It allows querying and updating an array extremely efficiently.

The Problem: Given an array of numbers, you need to frequently query the `Sum(L, R)` (or Min/Max) and frequently update individual elements `A[i] = X`.
— If you use a standard Array: Update is `O(1)`, but Range Query is `O(N)`.
— If you use a Prefix Sum Array: Range Query is `O(1)`, but Update is `O(N)` (you must rebuild the prefix array).

The Solution: A Segment Tree achieves O(log N) for BOTH Queries and Updates.
The leaves of the tree represent individual array elements. Each internal node represents the aggregated result (e.g., Sum) of its two children. To query a range, you traverse the tree, aggregating the pre-computed nodes that perfectly cover your range in `O(log N)` time.

Advanced variant: 'Lazy Propagation' allows you to update an entire range of elements `A[L...R] += X` in `O(log N)` time as well.

questions.q: What is the Fast & Slow Pointer technique (Floyd's Cycle Finding)?
questions.a:
Floyd's Tortoise and Hare algorithm is a two-pointer technique used primarily on Linked Lists to detect cycles using exactly `O(1)` extra space.

How it works:
Initialize two pointers, `Slow` and `Fast`, at the head of the list.
In a loop, move `Slow` by 1 step, and `Fast` by 2 steps.
— If `Fast` reaches `null`, the linked list has an end (No Cycle).
— If the linked list has a cycle, the `Fast` pointer will eventually lap the `Slow` pointer from behind. If `Slow == Fast`, a cycle is detected.

Finding the start of the cycle:
Once they meet, reset `Slow` back to the Head. Keep `Fast` at the meeting point. Now, move BOTH pointers by exactly 1 step at a time. The exact node where they collide again is mathematically guaranteed to be the start of the cycle.

questions.q: Explain Greedy Algorithms. When do they work?
questions.a:
A Greedy Algorithm builds a solution piece by piece, always choosing the next piece that offers the most immediate, local benefit (the 'greedy' choice). It never reconsiders or backtracks on previous choices.

When do they work?
They only yield the globally optimal solution if the problem exhibits the Greedy-Choice Property (a global optimum can be reached by a series of local optimums) and Optimal Substructure.

Examples where Greedy WORKS (Optimal):
— Dijkstra's Shortest Path.
— Kruskal's/Prim's Minimum Spanning Tree.
— Fractional Knapsack Problem (taking items by highest value/weight ratio).
— Huffman Coding (Data compression).

Examples where Greedy FAILS (Requires DP):
— 0/1 Knapsack Problem (you cannot break items; greedily taking the most valuable item might leave unusable empty space).
— Coin Change problem with arbitrary coin denominations (e.g., coins [1, 3, 4], target 6. Greedy takes 4+1+1 (3 coins). DP correctly finds 3+3 (2 coins)).

A Monotonic Stack is a standard Stack data structure that strictly maintains its elements in either increasing or decreasing order. It is a brilliant technique to solve 'Next Greater Element' array problems in O(N) time instead of `O(N²)`.

How to find the 'Next Greater Element' for every item in an array:
Iterate through the array. For the current element `X`:
While the stack is NOT empty AND `X` is greater than the element at the top of the stack:
— Pop the top element. You have just found the answer for the popped element (`X` is its next greater element).
Push `X` onto the stack.

Every element is pushed exactly once and popped exactly once, resulting in strict `O(N)` linear time.
Classic Problems: Daily Temperatures, Largest Rectangle in Histogram, Trapping Rain Water.

The Problem: Finding a substring pattern `P` (length M) inside a text `T` (length N). The naive brute-force approach takes `O(N * M)` because upon every mismatch, it moves the text pointer back and starts comparing from scratch.

KMP Solution: Achieves strictly O(N + M) time.
KMP avoids re-evaluating characters we have already seen. It pre-processes the pattern `P` to create an LPS Array (Longest Proper Prefix which is also a Suffix).
For example, in pattern `ABABC`, the prefix `AB` appears again as a suffix. The LPS array tells us exactly how much of the pattern we can safely 'skip' without missing a potential match.
When a mismatch occurs in the text, instead of resetting the text pointer, KMP uses the LPS array to shift the pattern pointer backward intelligently, keeping the text pointer constantly moving forward.

questions.q: What is a Hash Collision? How are they resolved?
questions.a:
A Hash Collision occurs when a Hash Function maps two completely different keys to the exact same array index (bucket) in a HashMap.

Since hash functions output a finite range of integers, but inputs are infinite, collisions are mathematically inevitable (Pigeonhole Principle).

Resolution Strategies:
1. Separate Chaining: Each array bucket holds a Pointer to a Linked List. If a collision occurs, the new key-value pair is simply appended to the list at that bucket. When retrieving, you hash to the bucket, then iterate the linked list checking `.equals()`. (Java uses this, and upgrades the list to a Red-Black tree if it exceeds 8 elements for `O(log N)` lookup).
2. Open Addressing (Linear Probing): All data is stored directly in the array. If a collision occurs at index `i`, the algorithm sequentially probes `i+1`, `i+2`, until it finds an empty slot. Extremely memory efficient (no node pointers), but suffers heavily from 'clustering' as the table gets full.

questions.q: What is tail recursion? Why is it useful?
questions.a:
Tail Recursion occurs when the recursive call is the absolute last operation performed in the function. No calculation is performed after the recursive call returns.

// Not Tail Recursive: Needs the result of factorial(n-1) to multiply by n
int fact(n) { if (n==0) return 1; return n * fact(n-1); }

// Tail Recursive: Passes the accumulated result forward
int fact(n, acc) { if (n==0) return acc; return fact(n-1, n * acc); }

Why it is useful: Modern compilers (C++, Scala, functional languages) recognize tail recursion and perform Tail Call Optimization (TCO).
Because no state needs to be saved after the recursive call, the compiler reuses the exact same stack frame for every call instead of pushing new ones. This transforms the recursion into a highly efficient `while` loop under the hood, completely eliminating the risk of `StackOverflowError` and taking `O(1)` space.
